exercises.title: Invariance — which assignments compile?
exercises.scenario: Generics are invariant — subtype relationships don't carry through.
exercises.expected_solution:
A: E
B: C
C: E
D: C
E: C

test_cases.expected:
A: E
B: C
C: E
D: C
E: C

hints: List<Integer> is NOT List<Number> — invariance
List<String> is NOT List<Object> — invariance
Number x = new Integer(42) — IS-A works for non-generic types
List<?> accepts ANY List — wildcard

exercises.title: Add ? extends for flexible parameter
exercises.scenario: Without wildcard: only List<Number>. With wildcard: any numeric list.
exercises.instructions: Fill in the blank to make sumList accept List<Integer>, List<Double>, and List<Number>.
hints: ? extends Number = any list of Number or subtype
Producer pattern: reading from list → extends

exercises.title: Unbounded wildcard for printAll
exercises.scenario: Unbounded ? accepts any list for read-only operations.
exercises.instructions:
Write `printAll(List<?> list)` that prints each element. Show it works for String, Integer, and Boolean lists.

Expected output:
[Aisha, Raj]
[1, 2, 3]
[true, false]
exercises.starter_code:
import java.util.*;
// printAll with unbounded wildcard
hints: List<?> accepts any parameterized List
list.toString() works — no knowledge of element type needed

exercises.title: ? extends for numeric processing
exercises.instructions:
Write `average(List<? extends Number>)` returning OptionalDouble. Test with Integer and Double lists.

Expected output:
84.33333333333333
2.5
exercises.starter_code:
import java.util.*;
// average with ? extends Number
hints: ? extends Number — reads as Number (doubleValue() available)
Same pattern as bounded type parameter T extends Number, but using wildcard

exercises.title: ? super for consumer (adding to list)
exercises.scenario: Lower bounded wildcard allows adding to compatible list.
exercises.instructions:
Write `addIntegers(List<? super Integer>, int... values)` that adds the values. Show it works for List<Integer>, List<Number>, and List<Object>.

Expected output:
[10, 20, 30]
[10, 20, 30]
[10, 20, 30]
exercises.starter_code:
import java.util.*;
// ? super Integer allows adding Integer to any compatible list
hints: ? super Integer = Integer, Number, or Object — any supertype of Integer
Consumer pattern: writing to list → super
All three list types can hold Integer values

exercises.title: PECS copy method
exercises.instructions:
Implement `copy(List<? super T> dest, List<? extends T> src)` that copies all elements from src to dest. Test copying a List<Integer> into a List<Number>.

Expected output:
Before: []
After: [1, 2, 3]
exercises.starter_code:
import java.util.*;
// PECS copy: ? extends = producer, ? super = consumer
hints: static <T> void copy(List<? super T> dest, List<? extends T> src)
src produces T (extends = producer); dest consumes T (super = consumer)
T=Integer inferred from source; List<Number> satisfies ? super Integer

exercises.title: Trace PECS compile/error analysis
exercises.scenario: Identify what operations are allowed with each wildcard.
exercises.expected_solution:
A: C
B: E
C: C
D: C
E: E
F: C

test_cases.expected:
A: C
B: E
C: C
D: C
E: E
F: C

hints: ? extends: read as T (Number), cannot add
? super: add T (Integer), read only as Object
size() works on any List regardless of wildcard

exercises.title: Wildcard with Collection — countIf
exercises.scenario: Count matching elements in any collection using wildcard.
exercises.instructions:
Write `countIf(Collection<? extends T>, Predicate<T>)` returning count of matching elements. Test with different collection types.

Expected output:
Integers > 5: 3
Strings starting with A: 2
hints: Collection<? extends T> accepts List<Integer>, Set<String> etc.
T is inferred from Predicate<T> argument
Integers > 5: 7, 8, 9 = count 3; Aisha, Anita start with A = count 2

exercises.title: ? extends for Shape hierarchy
exercises.scenario: Classic wildcard use case — process any list of shapes.
exercises.instructions:
Create `Shape` (abstract, area()), `Circle` (radius), `Rectangle` (w,h). Write `totalArea(List<? extends Shape>)`. Show it works for all three list types.

Expected output:
Circles total area: 78.54
Rectangles total area: 60.0
Mixed total area: 138.54
exercises.starter_code:
import java.util.*;
// Shape hierarchy + totalArea with ? extends Shape

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Circles total area: 78.54
Rectangles total area: 60.0
Mixed total area: 138.54

hints: totalArea(List<? extends Shape>) accepts List<Circle>, List<Rectangle>, List<Shape>
Reads each as Shape — area() method available

exercises.title: ? super for comparator — addAll with filter
exercises.instructions:
Write `copyFiltered(List<? super T>, List<? extends T>, Predicate<T>)` — copy elements matching predicate from source to destination.

Test: copy even integers from List<Integer> to List<Number>.

Expected output:
Source: [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]
Evens in target: [2, 4, 6]
hints: src = producer (? extends T); dest = consumer (? super T)
T inferred as Integer from source; List<Number> satisfies ? super Integer

exercises.title: Wildcard in return type — max of any numeric list
exercises.scenario: Method returning from a wildcard collection.
exercises.instructions:
Write `maxNumber(List<? extends Number>)` returning Optional<Number> — the maximum value. Use compareTo via doubleValue comparison.

Test with Integer and Double.

Expected output:
Max of ints: Optional[95]
Max of doubles: Optional[3.9]
Max of empty: Optional.empty
exercises.starter_code:
import java.util.*;
// maxNumber using ? extends Number

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Max of ints: Optional[95]
Max of doubles: Optional[3.9]
Max of empty: Optional.empty

hints: List<? extends Number> → reads each element as Number
Compare using .doubleValue() — Number method
Return type Optional<Number> — widened from the element type

exercises.title: Map with wildcard values
exercises.instructions:
Write `printValues(Map<String, ? extends Number> map)` that prints key=value pairs. Test with Map<String, Integer> and Map<String, Double>.

Expected output:
INFY=1560
RELIANCE=2450
TCS=3890
Pi=3.14
E=2.72
exercises.starter_code:
import java.util.*;
// Map with wildcard value type
hints: Map<String, ? extends Number> accepts Map<String, Integer> and Map<String, Double>
Values read as Number — .toString() called for printing
TreeMap sorts keys alphabetically

exercises.title: Complete merge method using PECS
exercises.scenario: Merge a collection of lists using producer/consumer wildcards.
exercises.instructions:
Write `merge(List<? super T> dest, List<? extends List<? extends T>> sources)` — merges multiple source lists into one destination list.

Test: merge three List<Integer> into a List<Number>.

Expected output:
[1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9]
exercises.starter_code:
import java.util.*;
// merge multiple source lists into destination using PECS
hints: dest is consumer: ? super T
sources is a list of producers: ? extends List<? extends T>
Nested wildcard: each source is itself a producer of T

exercises.title: PECS in a generic Sorter class
exercises.scenario: A generic sorter that uses wildcards correctly.
exercises.instructions:
Create `Sorter<T extends Comparable<T>>` class with:
- `sort(List<T>)` — sorts in place
- `sorted(List<? extends T>)` — returns new sorted list (producer wildcard)
- `static <E extends Comparable<E>> void sortAll(List<? extends List<E>>)` — sorts each inner list

Test all three.

Expected output:
In-place: [Aisha, Priya, Raj]
New sorted: [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
All sorted: [[Aisha, Priya], [Raj, Vikram]]
exercises.starter_code:
import java.util.*;
// Sorter<T> with multiple wildcard-based sort methods

test_cases.expected_stdout:
In-place: [Aisha, Priya, Raj]
New sorted: [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
All sorted: [[Aisha, Priya], [Raj, Vikram]]

hints: sorted(): List<? extends T> accepts List<Integer> when T=Integer
sortAll(): List<? extends List<E>> — each inner list is a producer
Collections.sort(copy) works because T is Comparable

exercises.title: Full wildcard utility — WildcardCollections
exercises.scenario: Six wildcard-based methods demonstrating all three wildcard forms.
exercises.instructions:
Build `WildcardCollections` with six methods:
1. `print(Collection<?>)` — print any collection
2. `sum(Collection<? extends Number>)` — sum any numbers
3. `max(Collection<? extends Comparable<?>>)` — max of any comparable collection (as Object)
4. `fill(List<? super String>, String[])` — add strings to any compatible list
5. `<T> copy(List<? super T>, List<? extends T>)` — PECS copy
6. `<T> merge(List<? super T>, Collection<? extends T>[])` — merge multiple sources

Demonstrate all 6 with test data.

Expected output:
[Aisha, Raj, Priya]
Sum: 60.0
Copied: [1, 2, 3]
Filled: [hello, world]
Merged: [a, b, c, d, e, f]

test_cases.expected_stdout:
[Aisha, Raj, Priya]
Sum: 60.0
Copied: [1, 2, 3]
Filled: [hello, world]
Merged: [a, b, c, d, e, f]

hints: print: ? — any collection
sum: ? extends Number — numeric producer
fill: ? super String — string consumer
copy: PECS — ? extends T src, ? super T dest
merge: varargs Collection<? extends T>[] sources
